item.title: IAS invests in improving rehabilitation and support services for the elderly
item.description:
The Social Action Institute will move forward this year with two ten-year plans that aim to raise the level of rehabilitation and support services for the elderly. The body will review the system for granting subsidies to social service institutions, but will also finance the acquisition of rehabilitation technologies in these social facilities. Director Hon Wai also revealed that the registration program for isolated elderly people is almost finished, the data collected from which will be used to create a database to support the elderly.
Improving elderly support and rehabilitation services will be the focus of work for the Institute of Social Action (IAS) this year, with the launch of two ten-year plans. According to Hon Wai, president of the IAS, among the planned measures, a review of the regime for granting subsidies to social service institutions will be carried out, which will serve to increase the effectiveness of services and create a greater innovative capacity of these social facilities. The IAS will also launch a financing program for the acquisition of rehabilitation technologies that aims to encourage the use of these technologies in social service institutions, Hon Wai revealed yesterday during a spring lunch for workers in the social service sector. It is recalled that the second Ten-Year Action Plan for Support Services for the Elderly and the second Ten-Year Action Plan for Rehabilitation Services will be implemented this year. The last two ten-year plans ended last year, with the elderly plan covering topics such as Medical and Social Assistance Services, Guarantee of Rights, Social Participation and Living Environment. Rehabilitation encompassed 13 service areas, including medical rehabilitation, development of self-help organizations for disabled people and construction of a barrier-free environment, among others. Still on social services for the elderly, the IAS, with the coordination of more than 70 social service institutions, is collecting data as part of the work “Survey and Registration of Isolated Elderly People and Families of Two Elderly People”, whose work is “about to be completed”, said the person in charge. Hon Wai highlighted that, upon completion of data collection, a database will be established that will serve as a tool for defining support measures for the elderly, as well as creating “15-Minute Service Circles for the Elderly”. The aforementioned register of isolated elderly people is a general survey to be conducted among isolated elderly people and families of elderly couples, through which the IAS hopes to better understand the basic situation and service needs of isolated elderly people and families of two elderly people in Macau. The registration is expected to involve 34,000 senior local residents. In addition to measures aimed at the aging population, the IAS addressed the issue of the low birth rate and promised to implement measures to support the areas of child care services. According to Hon Wai, this year, the IAS and the services involved will cooperate in the launch of the “Full Cycle Health Protection Plan for Pregnant and Postpartum Women” and in carrying out several courses aimed at guardians, with a view to creating a “social support system that covers the entire cycle and all aspects of residents' lives”, he stressed. The official also recalled that the Government launched the Child Care Subsidy for the first time with the aim of encouraging birth rates and providing financial support to families with babies. At the same time, emotional education for children was introduced in childcare services and specific training is provided for daycare workers. “Social Service is, in fact, a fundamental pillar for protecting the well-being of the entire population of the region and long-term social development”, defended Hon Wai in his speech for the event. He said he hopes that, through the decentralization of resources in social action and the reinforcement of support for vulnerable groups, effective social action measures will be implemented and a solid base of support for the population's subsistence will be guaranteed.

item.title: “Let’s all be inhabitants of a theme park”
item.description:
If there was a lesbianism pandemic, what would happen? It is from this absurd and humorous premise that the book “O Desfufador” begins, populated by surreal characters and with a chapter dedicated to Macau. As part of the Macau Literary Festival, literary journalist, Sara Figueiredo Costa, and the author, Valério Romão, presented the book, yesterday, at Livraria Portuguesa.
In “O Desfufador – Volume 1: Contagio”, the environment is created for the action that will take place in the second volume, but this “is not a prologue book”, warns Sara Figueiredo Costa. “The history and biography of the characters are presented”, he summarizes, but “it’s worth it in itself”. However, he leaves everything on hold for a second book, which, according to the author, Valério Romão, will arrive later this year. The tone of “O Desfufador” is admittedly humorous. “The first chapter introduces us to a child called Alexandre who, during the filming of the film ‘Recordações da Casa Amarela’, by João César Monteiro, gets kicked in the ass and becomes dwarfed”, says the author. In a very different tone than usual, Valério Romão explains the boldness. “In this stupid and incomprehensible world, the best way to deal with this general air was humor,” he says. The idea came about after reading, a long time ago, about a dancing epidemic in Strasbourg, which resulted in the deaths of hundreds of people. The pandemic in “O Desfufador” goes a little further. “It calls into question a man's identity based on a woman's desire and I thought this was very good”, says the author. The difficulty of humor
It's difficult to do humor. Aware that the style may not suit everyone, Valério is not worried about criticism. “There were people who said it was a series of nonsense, but the truth is that this language exists on the street, it is not reflected in literature and, if it is not written down, it disappears from memory”, he states. Sara asks: “Can’t humor deal with serious issues?” Valério disagrees. “There is a band of revolutionaries who decided to form NALGA to counter the Thaiization movement in Lisbon and their big idea is to sink an ocean liner and drive away tourists”, he describes. With humor, it deals with a serious issue, which is gentrification. “We are all going to be inhabitants of a theme park”, he emphasizes. Macau's role In a totally surreal scenario, the territory is not left out, with a chapter dedicated entirely to it. “I fell in love with Macau, with Chinese culture in the broadest sense, with the Chinese language, with this civilization”, he adds. Therefore, he decided to include the territory, giving “a small glimpse” of what the city is, from the perspective of someone who understands nothing about Chinese culture. As for the second volume, it is already being prepared. It will be ready this year, but it is not yet known whether Macau will enter again.

item.title: “It was the uncovering of what was covered by history” — the documentary that gave the last Salatinas their voice back
item.description:
There were those who cried on the phone, those who didn't want to touch the wound eighty years later. But there were also those who opened their front doors, showed yellowed photographs and sat at the table to tell stories of an Alta de Coimbra that no longer exists. The last “Salatinas”, survivors of the demolition of the old alta in the forties and fifties, are now the protagonists of a documentary that gives them back their dignity and memory. Filipa Queiroz, Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira were with them, listened to them and filmed them. The result is an intimate portrait of a wound still open in the country's history.
The “Salatinas” are the last survivors of the community in the old town of Coimbra. How did you find them and how did you build the trust necessary for them to share such personal memories and photographs with you? Rafael Vieira: It was a very long process to get into people's privacy, especially because some people have good memories, but there are traumatic memories too, of expulsion. We started this process in the context of a magazine to do a report, then it turned into a double report that was even awarded in a magazine in Coimbra in 2022! And since it was so well received, I realized that it made sense to turn it into a book. This process brought me closer to people, some opened other doors for us and became facilitators for other people. The book, when produced, also opened up other interests. Other people approached, also wanting to talk, comment and share their memories. Then we decided to move on to the film. I met Filipa, who was my editor at that magazine, we called Tiago, and we realized that the film made sense. We matured the project and this maturity led to us contacting dozens and dozens of people. The film is the result of this sum of maturation, contact and work.
Filipa Queiroz: It's a living story too, it never really ended. Even now, every time we present the film, there are people who contact us, who go to the screenings and who always add something. Once there is a report, a book and a film, things seem to grow.
Rafael Vieira: There are several levels, it grew like a snowball.
Was there a specific story from one of the interviewees that changed your perspective on what this documentary would become? Filipa Queiroz: All of them, in a certain way. Not changing it per se, but adding without a doubt. There was a lot that we discovered throughout this research, because it wasn't just capturing something that was already accomplished, it was actually adding points. I think we had several moments that may not have changed the thread, but that forced us to add them because they were interesting. It wasn't all designed from the beginning, it was made.
Rafael Vieira: There was also academic support. Three academics speak in the film, one from the musical side, one from the art history side and Nuno Rosmaninho, who was the person who investigated the social issue the most, the issue of property owners. And this academic support allowed us to look for other material. I remember several moments when we were surprised with information, with complaints to the State, quite violent complaints in an authoritarian context, very emotional letters... People were on the verge of not being able to contest the loss of their property... they were expelled. The compensation to which they were entitled was much lower than their expectations and the market value... there is a whole traumatic process. The people who were expelled and placed in the peripheral neighborhoods of Coimbra were more disadvantaged people. The owners who were at an economic level that allowed a much more complete, more dignified life, were left without money, without possessions.
Filipa Queiroz: Seeing these letters and looking through them in the archive, thanks to the help of the person in the archive who not only opened the doors for us but was proactive in finding more things, it ended up being a collective work, people got involved. We discovered an unreleased album by an artist of the time, well known in the Salatina community, who later fell into oblivion. And suddenly we have original music from the 1920s, which the family itself provided, proud to showcase. It was another layer that we didn't expect to include.
Tiago Cerveira: More than ten or fifteen interviews, more than photographs, there are videos of someone who at the time had the sensitivity to record his own destruction. This is what allows these types of films, to combine orality with the image.
Rafael Vieira: The film ends up revealing these hidden treasures.
Filipa Queiroz: This is also very revealing of our three different profiles. A videographer, a journalist and an architect come together and one is super focused on architectural heritage, another on video materials and another on cultural materials.
ELOI CARVALHO PHOTOGRAPHY You were able to find previously unseen footage of the actual demolitions and a rare recording from the 1920s. Can you explain some of the detective work involved in discovering these files? Where were all these treasures hidden? Rafael Vieira: It’s a lot of archeology work. The film brings together research in public, institutional, university and municipal archives, Diário de Coimbra reserves and personal collections. There are people who have boxes that were passed down from grandparents, to parents, to children, which contain many things that people no longer even remember. There are people who have archives that occupy the room, others gather their memories in a shoebox, but each photograph evokes a memory they have, memories of the Velha Alta of Coimbra that they took with them to the new neighborhoods where they were installed. We managed to capture this, these people remembering what is possible.
Rafael Vieira: The head of each of them is a living archive that needs to be unraveled. It's extraordinary. A central person passed away after the film was released, unfortunately, and with each person who disappears, a file is lost. It is urgent to encapsulate in film a story of common affections, our ancestry is Salatina.
Filipa Queiroz: My grandmother is one of the protagonists of the documentary, she is 96 years old now and the differences can already be seen since she was interviewed. She was not interviewed because she was displaced, fortunately, but she was born and still lives in Alta de Coimbra, in a place that was not affected by the demolitions. He has a very vivid, and above all descriptive, memory of what the Old Alta of Coimbra was like and the things that happened there.
The documentary is structured into three distinct chapters: vibrant life, destruction and dispersion. Was the idea always to conceive it in this triptych format or did this structure emerge during the editing process? Tiago Cerveira: We can try to follow a path, but after following that path it completely turns when we feel that we are missing other layers, the music, the practices. But we managed to come up with a film that does justice to the community that existed there, a completely heterogeneous community. They were professors, bohemian students, tricanas, futricas, musicians, people with less social status and people with more social status. We listened to everyone, we talked to everyone.
Filipa Queiroz: We also added a narrative part based on Rafael's book. We tailored the thing because we didn’t want it to just be, as Tiago says, “Portugal sitting down”.
Rafael Vieira: We wanted it to be an orange, given to someone from Lisbon and peeled, even though they didn't know anything about Coimbra. Portugal knows that there is a University of Coimbra, but even though we don't know anything about the city, the history is clear when you peel it back.
How did you balance the need to be historically accurate with the desire to tell an emotionally engaging story? Tiago Cerveira: We never wanted to do an inventory. We wanted to hear the last living Salatinas and we realized that some would end up being left out, otherwise we would have film for ten or fifteen hours. There had to be a commitment to create an audiovisual product that told a story, but that did not become a cult product for those who already know it. It's not a film by Salatinas for Salatinas.
Filipa Queiroz: We wanted it to have an impact on people and we are very happy with the results. Now there are young students reporting on the subject, they invite them to talk about the subject, they approach the Salatinas themselves, who ended up emerging in the community in a different way and with another prominence. They feel seen and proud. We are talking about people whose family members committed suicide because of what happened. It's brutal, really brutal.
Rafael Vieira: It's not repairing history, that's impossible. It happened, it has to be told, and it was never done. The university itself is aware of this issue of people not being heard and is creating an interpretive center. They call it an “interpretive portal”, it is located on the threshold of what was destroyed. We contribute to this too.
ELOI CARVALHO PHOTOGRAPHY For the “Salatinas” who are still alive, what was their reaction to seeing their old neighborhood and their stories on the big screen? Tiago Cerveira: Smiles, tears… The Salatinas themselves said that they ended up learning a lot of things about the community. In Coimbra we sold out the two sessions we had, 1,200 people. There are Portuguese films that don't fill up in ten, fifteen, thirty viewings. Here in Macau it will be the first exhibition outside of Coimbra, it will be the first test for the general public.
Filipa Queiroz: We had a Salatina, Maria Luísa, who told us that if she had known she was going to appear on such a big screen she would have dressed better, she didn't think the film would have so much size. She simply opened her doors, everyone was so receptive, but they weren't expecting to be seen afterwards with such dignity, with such an impact. There are more women than men and that is also something that makes me very happy, that women have a voice. They love it, they are very happy.
Rafael Vieira: The star of what remained is total pride, fascination and descendants wanting to know more. It was the uncovering of what was covered by history.
Survivors are at the center of this film. How did you guide the interviews to get someone to open up about an eighty-year trauma without exploring their pain? How did you draw the line between journalist and confidant? Tiago Cerveira: The basis of everything, since for many people it was their first interview, is never creating an interview atmosphere. It's a conversation, a super down-to-earth approach to create a family atmosphere. We went to people's homes to record them and when people feel at home they feel completely different at ease.
Filipa Queiroz: The conversations had ups and downs.
Rafael Vieira: How it was an exercise of violence on the part of the State is always a traumatic issue, but there is a distance that… lightens this trauma. We contacted two people, descendants of owners, who did not want to talk. One of them was particularly difficult because she cried on the phone, she didn't want to touch it. They had free lives, in economic terms, and lost everything. There are people who did not overcome this process, they do not appear in the film.
Filipa Queiroz: Those who appear in the film spoke very comfortably, with some sadness at times, but this did not prevent them from continuing the interview. And we, of course, always with the greatest respect. We don't explore tears, we don't explore sadness and pain. This is surprising for those who haven't seen it, but the film has several moments of humor and laughter. This was also surprising for us, in a very organic and natural way there is a balance. It's like life, we all have traumas, then it depends on how we live with them. Fortunately, the Salatinas with whom we were able to speak are people who have overcome what happened to them, who want to share and accept that pain exists, but they do not experience it with any burden and transmit it.
Tiago Cerveira: It's a catharsis.
Rafael Vieira: It's not that the filming process was therapeutic for them, but they revisit childhood memories and it's their childhood that they are recovering, and obviously we always remember happy moments from childhood.
And how is life after the film's release? What are the plans for him now? Filipa Queiroz: We held sharing sessions in a student republic and at the university archive they called us to talk about the film. We did two “Salatine walks”, two guided tours of the Old Alta of Coimbra, with full capacity, where we talked about the film and our findings in a very sensorial way because we took some objects we found, audios, videos, little pieces of the film. E, entretanto, tem sido aquele caminho de enviar o filme para vários sítios, desde candidaturas para festivais de cinema, até cineteatros. All sessions are free, we have always done this because the film is not a commercial product and the idea will always be to reach the internet, then television, if we can. Toda a gente de Coimbra pergunta se vamos vender, se vamos fazer DVDs, e nós dizemos sempre que sim. Se houver sempre momentos de partilha e de conversa, melhor.
Tiago Cerveira: We're going to Copenhagen, Lisbon, Porto and Rio de Janeiro.
Rafael Vieira: We will also be at the University of São José giving a three-hour class with architecture students. Let's relate with the Lai Chi Vun shipyards, in Coloane. There was a process of transformation, not necessarily expulsion, but there is a social and community transformation there that is interesting to build a bridge with Coimbra. Let's do some work with them on architectural arguments and perception of the social environment.

item.title: Seoul and Washington military exercises threaten stability
item.description: Kim Yo-jong, sister of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, said ongoing military exercises between South Korea and the United States pose a threat to regional stability. In a statement cited yesterday by the North Korean state news agency KCNA, the influential leader said that the maneuvers constitute a “provocative and aggressive war game”, which could “have terrible and unimaginable consequences”. The country's “enemies”, South Korea and the United States, “should never test our patience”, warned the sister of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un. The annual Freedom Shield exercise will result in “further destruction of regional stability” in a context of “recent global geopolitical crises,” Kim Yo-jong said. The exercises are taking place “at a critical time, when the global security structure is rapidly collapsing and wars are breaking out in different parts of the world”, added the leader. Kim Yo-jong attributed the situation to the “reckless actions of outrageous international bandits,” in an apparent reference to the United States and Israel. The sister of the leader of North Korea condemned the attacks against Iran as an “illegal act of aggression” that revealed the nature of Washington, which she described as dishonest. Pyongyang concluded a crucial single-party congress in February, during which it reaffirmed the isolated country's nuclear program, amid promises to expand its nuclear arsenal. Kim Yo-jong stated that the Asian country will “constantly and repeatedly” convince adversaries of its “military deterrence capacity and its inevitability”. North Korea has reacted harshly in the past to these maneuvers, which it considers a rehearsal for an invasion of its territory and a demonstration of the hostile policy of Washington and Seoul. This year's exercises began on Monday, with the participation of around 18,000 South Korean troops and an as-yet unknown number of US troops, and will continue until March 19. Described as defensive by both countries, U.S. Forces Korea said the exercises include live ammunition drills, simulations and field exercises designed to improve joint operational readiness.

item.title: DSEDJ moves away from easing restrictions on non-local students
item.description: The Directorate of Education and Youth Development Services (DSEDJ) has no intention, for now, of easing restrictions on non-local students. In response to Congresswoman Song Pek Kei's suggestion, the authorities explain that, in order to have this flexibility, it will first be necessary to assess the long-term impact of the measure, as the issuance of student visas is at stake, and “factors such as the form of residence in Macau of students' companions and their authorizations, among other aspects, must be considered”. It should be remembered that non-local students are currently prohibited from non-higher education in Macau. DSEDJ also says that, in the future, new ways should be established for students from mainland China to continue their higher studies in Macau. In the written interpellation, Song Pek Kei pointed out that, “currently, proposals are emerging in society that defend the opening of admission of non-local students to non-higher education schools” in Macau. In view of this, the deputy asked the Government if it would carry out an “in-depth study of the feasibility of this suggestion”, in order to “transform existing educational resources into a driving force for development, thus contributing to Macau building itself as a ‘hub’ for outstanding international talents”. The deputy linked to the Fujian community also called for the promotion of exemption from the professional qualification exam for Macau teachers for the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin. In its response, DSEDJ explained that the General Development Plan of the In-depth Cooperation Zone between Guangdong and Macau in Hengqin proposes that teachers from Macau, “who are qualified to teach in kindergarten, primary or secondary schools and who demonstrate love for the country and Macau, can carry out duties on the Mountain Island. On the other hand, the general framework for teaching staff in non-tertiary private schools also stipulates that Macau residents can benefit from the same benefits that exist in schools in Macau when they teach in schools intended for Macao SAR students in the Cooperation Zone, as well as receive the allowance for professional development intended for Macau teachers and calculate their seniority through the registration of teaching staff in the DSEDJ.

item.title: In the middle of Cotai, stands a chapel of tranquility
item.description:
The Capella hotel opened last February, around two years after it was announced and more than five years after the first sketches, signed by Moinard Bétaille, were designed. The years of maturation resulted in a project marked by architectural sophistication and a common concept throughout the space, characterized by earth tones and illustrations inspired by the dreamlike refuge of the jungle. Yesterday, Galaxy Macau organized a guided tour of the space and presented to the territory's press the “rare combination of grandeur, serenity and exclusivity” that distinguishes the group's ninth hotel.
When you enter through the doors of Capella, you leave behind the cars tearing up the tarmac and the unrest of Cotai and enter a kind of dome of serenity. The hotel's seventeen floors are supported by a trunk-shaped structure that seems to irrigate life from the soil to the green exuberance of the jungle, materialized by a small artificial lake with several sculptures of birds, turtles and aquatic plants. Surrounding the elevators, a huge work of digital art deepens the immersive experience with voluminous waterfalls, vegetation swaying in the wind and the chirping of immaterial birds. The completion of the hotel is faithful to the sketches presented in July last year by Moinard Bétaille, a prestigious interior design agency that combines the vision of Frenchman Bruno Moinard with that of his creative partner Claire Bétaille. On the occasion, regarding a retrospective exhibition of his three decades of career at GalaxyArt, Moinard confessed that Capella was born from the intention of planting a natural oasis in the most cosmopolitan area of ​​Macau. “We entered the elevators as if we were under a rock, in a cave”, he revealed, at the time, when the hotel still did not have a concrete opening date. “As soon as we access the entrance, we are in the jungle.” In fact, the elevator to the first floor is an ascension to the densest areas of nature. The sun's rays slide discreetly into the lobby, but do not impose their presence: a feeling of serene refuge is maintained on the soft-lined, canopy-shaped ceilings and on the undulating walls where brushstrokes of trees and tropical plants grow. The Capella presentation statement, issued in 2024, indicated that the interiors drew inspiration from the placid greens of the “jungle” and the “verdant hills of Macau”. If the description of the city adopts a certain poetic freedom, the incorporation of these elements in the design of the Galaxy's latest project – the ninth hotel in total – was done absolutely. From the first to the seventeenth floor, the thread of beige and brown tones pulses, illustrations of abundant vegetation and the discreet opulence that characterizes the works of Parisian Moinard Bétaille. The refinement of details and pieces of art specially commissioned for the hotel coexist, however, with the promise of intimacy and familiarity in an exotic environment. It is based on this concept that the typical reception is replaced by a “living room” – a social lounge where guests can enjoy personalized service with tea ceremonies, alcoholic liquor tastings and cultural presentations. Still on the first floor, the highlights are the Botanica restaurant and the Pony & Plume bar, both examples of excellence in their respective areas. Botanica serves international dishes infused with Asian influences, suited to the palates of travelers from around the world, and continues the hotel's theme with red oak carvings and vines stretching from the walls to the ceilings.
Pony & Plume serves a different concept – also inspired by nature, but with greater sophistication and a certain cinematic quality. The title alludes to the “pony”, a unit of measurement for alcohol, but the aesthetics of the space pay homage to equids and classic Western films. At the counter, the most popular scene – at least among young people with cell phones in hand and the “influencers” who visit the space. An eight-level tower made up of hundreds of bottles of whiskey, from the smoothest and most aromatic to the full-bodied and smoky.
THE BIGGEST ROOMS IN SOUTH CHINA
The grandeur of the rooms is literal: they are, after all, “the largest rooms in southern China”, according to Galaxy in a statement. They are also the highlight of the property. Available in two- and four-bedroom versions, the 'penthouses' offer an expansive space that integrates the external and internal environments, separated by sliding doors to isolate the room from the cold or let in the sun depending on the temperatures. For moments of greater contemplation, terraces, sun loungers and private infinity-edge pools were designed, overlooking the rest of the Galaxy's properties (and the futuristic setting of Hengqin's skyscrapers, further in the background). As for entertainment, options range from foosball tables, chessboards and gym equipment to a private, soundproofed karaoke room. The two-bedroom version, designed for families, also includes a space dedicated to younger guests and personalized according to their preferences. The one- and two-bedroom suites do not have the famous infinity pools – instead they have an indoor relaxation pool, window loungers and marble bathtubs. Interior design reaches its apex in the guest rooms, which are accented with hand-painted wall coverings by artist LaLa Curio. When the visit comes to an end, the digital jungle on the first floor still continues to show images of water flows and birds crossing the skies, but it is possible to discover something new: a bright star among pastel-colored clouds. It's dusk. At night, the birds and vegetation will give way to fireflies and shooting stars.

item.title: Si Ka Lon suggests greater cooperation between Macau and Portuguese-speaking countries in the technology industry
item.description:
Deputy Si Ka Lon says that the Government should facilitate the arrival of qualified staff to Macau so that there is a greater number of scientific-technological cooperation projects with Portuguese-speaking countries.
To “transform the results of the articulation into lasting and sustainable industrial implementations” and “prepare the basis for the future Macau Scientific and Industrial Park to be able to create a closed circuit effect capable of attracting, retaining and developing projects”, the Government must provide more incentives and guarantees in international cooperation mechanisms and in facilitating the residence of qualified staff. The proposal was presented to the Government by deputy Si Ka Lon in a written interpellation. For the deputy, this reinforcement of cooperation measures would ensure the “realization of a greater number of high-quality scientific-technological cooperation projects between Portuguese-speaking countries and Macau, including in the Deep Cooperation Zone between Macau and Zhuhai (Hengqin)”. The deputy linked to the Fujian community recalls that the Fund for the Development of Science and Technology of Macau (FDCT) reached, in 2023, a consensus with the Foundation for Science and Technology of Portugal on the renewal of the joint financing agreement, however, the process has been postponed. It has also not yet been formally renewed or started a new round of co-financing, “as our Fund, through the Financial Support Program for Cooperation in Science and Technology with Abroad, and the Fundação Paulista de Educação e Tecnologia de S. Paulo are jointly financing cooperation with Portuguese-speaking countries”. Therefore, Si Ka Lon argues that there is “a need to expand and reinforce cooperation channels with Portuguese-speaking countries”. In the interpellation, the deputy elected indirectly also points out that concrete measures have not yet been taken within the scope of the Development Plan for the Adequate Diversification of the Economy of the Macao SAR (2024-2028), which proposes exploring the granting of appropriate temporary stay authorizations to foreign innovation and technology teams, namely from Brazil and Portugal. Si Ka Lon therefore asks how, with the entry into force of the law on investment funds, the authorities will create a platform for Sino-Portuguese articulation of investment and financing in the technological field, “with a view to fully exercising the role of financial services in supporting the real economy and promoting technological innovation”. The deputy also questions the status of the cooperation agreement with the Foundation for Science and Technology of Portugal and whether the authorities will resort to the Financial Support Program for Cooperation in Science and Technology with Abroad or other incentive mechanisms, “with the aim of supporting Macau institutions in carrying out a greater number of collaborative projects with research teams from other Portuguese-speaking countries”. Finally, Si Ka Lon also asks for details on the timing of plans to implement the measure granting temporary stay authorizations to foreign scientific and technological innovation teams, namely from Brazil and Portugal, “so that they can develop entrepreneurial activities in the Macau-Hengqin region”.

item.title: China's exports increase almost 22% in January and February
item.description:
China's exports increased by almost 22% in the first two months of 2026 compared to the same period the previous year. Imports in January and February increased by almost 20%, above the 5.7% increase recorded in December.
China's exports rose nearly 22% in the first two months of 2026 compared to the same period a year earlier, as trade with countries other than the United States (US) expanded. Export figures released by China's General Administration of Customs were much better than economists predicted, far exceeding the 6.6% annual growth rate recorded in December. Imports in January and February increased by almost 20%, above the 5.7% increase recorded in December. However, China's imports from the US fell nearly 27% from the previous year. China's exports have been a bright spot for the economy despite tensions with the US. Chinese exports rose 5.5% in 2025, with the trade surplus reaching a record of almost 1.2 trillion dollars. Increased shipments to other regions, including Europe and Latin America, helped offset a 20% drop in exports to the US as US President Donald Trump imposed a series of higher tariffs on imports from much of the world. China's global trade surplus in January and February was $213.6 billion. Business data is typically combined for January and February each year to help balance the seasonal impacts of the Lunar New Year festival, the biggest holiday period of the year. The slowdown in the domestic economy, fueled by a multi-year recession in the real estate sector, has weighed on the world's second-largest economy. Last week, Chinese leaders announced an economic growth target of 4.5% to 5% for 2026, the lowest since 1991. The war in the Middle East has increased uncertainty about the prospects for trade, as well as China's own energy security. An effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a transit point for much of the world's oil and gas trade, could restrict China's access to relatively cheap Iranian oil and also reduce trade with the region. A recent U.S. Supreme Court ruling against Trump's sweeping tariffs, which has already resulted in lower rates for countries like China, could likely "provide modest support for Chinese exports," economists at Bank of America wrote in a research note. Trump's planned visit to Beijing at the end of March is being closely watched due to a possible extension of the trade truce between the two countries reached in October, which could be positive news for Chinese exports to the US. Lusa

item.title: Chinese courts handled 31,000 cases related to the SAR and Taiwan
item.description:
Chinese courts completed the trial of about 31,000 cases involving the SAR and Taiwan in 2025, an increase of 15.6% year on year, according to a work report of the Supreme People's Court released yesterday at the National People's Congress. Zhang Jun, president of China's judicial body, highlighted that he had promoted judicial coordination in the Greater Bay Area and invited mediation organizations from Hong Kong and Macau to handle almost a thousand arbitration cases.
Last year, Chinese courts handled around 31,000 cases involving the special administrative regions and Taiwan, representing an increase of 15.6% compared to the previous year. The data were provided in a work report by China's highest court, presented yesterday at a plenary meeting of the fourth session of the 14th National People's Congress (NPC), in Beijing. Zhang Jun, president of the Supreme People's Court of China, highlighted in his report that the body "protected the legitimate rights and interests" of residents of the SAR and Taiwan, as well as "upheld equity and justice and safeguarded a high quality of life through high-level judicial work". The report noted that over the past year, the articulation of judicial rules in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area has been promoted. According to the judge, arbitration awards are already recognized and enforced in cases where companies, financed by Hong Kong and Macau and established in the nine cities of the Greater Bay Area, chose Hong Kong or Macau as the place of arbitration. He further added that Guangdong courts last year invited mediation organizations from Hong Kong and Macau to arbitrate nearly a thousand cases involving disputes involving elements from Hong Kong, Macau and abroad. Zhang Jun also highlighted the participation of Taiwanese professionals in judicial activities within China, with the fact that the Mainland invited 129 mediators from Taiwan to participate in the Taiwan-related mediation platform, which “effectively resolved disputes” regarding the protection of the rights and interests of Taiwanese citizens and enterprises. Regarding the work organization for this year, the Supreme People's Court report said it will place greater emphasis on collaboration between the rule of law and reform, development and stability. “It will pay more attention to safeguarding and promoting equity and social justice”, and strengthen the protection of the rights and interests of citizens of the SAR and Taiwan. The prosecutorial bodies, in turn, concluded 2,180 cases against citizens of the SAR and Taiwan, as well as Chinese nationals residing in other parts of the world. In his work report, also presented at the same APN meeting, Ying Yong, attorney general of the Supreme State People's Procuratorate, pointed out that he has deepened exchanges and cooperation with the police and judicial authorities of Hong Kong and Macau.
APN guarantees close connection with the RAE
On the same occasion, the work report of Zhao Leji, president of the NPC Standing Committee, was also heard, who also highlighted the connection with the SARs throughout the past year. The third member in the hierarchy of the Politburo Standing Committee, the highest decision-making body of the Communist Party of China, reported that, in the fiscal and economic sphere, thematic research was carried out on initiatives such as the development of the Greater Bay Area. Zhao Leji assured that he had reinforced support for the performance of duties by APN members, especially those from Macau and Hong Kong, through “specialized seminars”, in order to ensure that representatives are well informed about issues relating to society and politics.
COMPLIMENTS FROM EVERYONE
Macau's APN representatives all expressed positive reactions to the reports presented. Vong Hin Fai, also president of the Macau Bar Association, praised the work of Chinese judicial bodies on Macau and Hong Kong, particularly with regard to the comprehensive work on the alignment and integration of judicial rules. Speaking to the Chinese channel Radio Macau, the lawyer pointed out that, in recent years, Macau has strived to take advantage of its “unique advantage of connecting the world” to serve Mainland companies that are expanding to Portuguese-speaking countries under the ‘One Belt, One Route’ initiative. Vong also said he believes that Macau should make more efforts in terms of arbitration and mediation. Iong Weng Ian highlighted the “remarkable results” achieved by the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate in terms of maintaining social stability, especially in combating cross-border crime and anti-corruption efforts. The vice-president of the General Women's Association cited the report as saying that in China, more than 40,000 cases involving cross-border fraud crimes in telecommunications originating in northern Myanmar were detected. He also highlighted that the resolution of corruption cases last year in China drew public attention and served as a “powerful wake-up call”.

item.title: The Hotel Central in English literature
item.description: The Hotel Central in Macau has inspired some writers of English-language literature over the decades. Yesterday, on a late afternoon visit, on the terrace of this space in the city, part of the Macau Literary Festival, historian Paul French and journalist Anne Marie Evans recovered the words of some authors, who passed through there or who used it for the setting of some of their books. Ian Fleming, author of the classic James Bond, visited the Hotel Central for the first time in 1959, in a work commissioned by The Sunday Times. He described it as “a nine-story skyscraper” and “the tallest building in Macau”, dedicated only “to human vices”. But he wasn't the only one to do so. Han Suyin, Ernest Gann and Richard Mason also included it in their books, as writer Paul French and journalist Anne Marie Evans told a group of Portuguese, Chinese and other nationalities. “The Hotel opened in 1928 and was the Hotel Presidente”, begins by explaining Paul French. Two years later, the name changed to Hotel Central. “When it opened, it had seven floors”, he recalls, adding that in the 1930s they added floors and, in 1942, while the world was struggling with the Second World War, a terrace. “Macau was neutral in this conflict,” he says. With the arrival of many people from Hong Kong and southern China, this space was expanded to include restaurants, a ballroom and gaming tables. At the request of The Sunday Times, which asked him to write after a trip to the most exciting cities, Ian Fleming chose Macau to be part of this itinerary. “He had to go to Hong Kong, because it was a British colony, but he got bored and ended up in Macau”, says the historian. In the book “Thrilling Cities”, published in 1963, Ian Fleming describes the Hotel Central. “The higher up you go in the building, the prettier and more expensive the girls are, the higher the stakes at the gaming tables and the better the music,” reads Anne Marie Evans. “Ian Fleming believed he was a great player”, comments French, who mentions that, when he goes up to the Hotel, he ends up describing a dance scene. “We danced for another hour, during which Garbo told me I looked like Stewart Granger and danced like Fred Astaire,” reads Evans. At the end, he describes leaving the space, referring to the hotel as “a nine-story palace with a bad reputation.” “Soldier of Fortune” by Ernest Gann The book “Soldier of Fortune”, by Ernest Gann, gave rise to a film, with Clark Gable. “It represents the perspective of a dangerous colony and portrays the Portuguese as dubious,” says French. The protagonist, Jane Hoyt, tries to save her husband, who was kidnapped in communist China and comes across a Portuguese man who runs a brothel under the façade of a language school and who manages to deceive her, stealing her money. In both the book and the film, there are descriptions of the Hotel Central. “It had a lobby on the first floor and a restaurant on the mezzanine,” says French. While Macau workers there played fan-tan, there was “a smell of cheap sweet wine and anxiety, which led to losing more money”. The hotel rooms extended over the next three floors and on top of it all there was a nightclub and “a Chinese band, which played American jazz”. “The Hill of Longing”, by Han Suyin Launched in 1952, in “A Colina da Saudade”, by Han Suyin, the main character ends a marriage with a general from the Kuomintang (Chinese Nationalist Party). While in Hong Kong, he ends up having an illicit relationship with a married reporter. “They cannot maintain a transparent relationship because British Hong Kong would not tolerate it,” comments French. One weekend in Macau, when they were heading to the Havana Hotel (which was, in fact, the Hotel Central), the author mentions “a tall and imposing building”, as Anne Marie Evans reads, with two receptionists behind a desk, one Portuguese and one Chinese. When describing the hotel, the protagonist mentions the existence of a gaming room on the top floor and mentions “concubines, servants, pirates and policemen”. “The World of Suzie Wong”, by Richard Mason “I always thought of this book as being about Hong Kong, but it includes a part about Macau,” says French of “The World of Suzie Wong,” a 1957 book by Richard Mason. Richard Lomax, the main character, is a British man who survives the Second World War and ends up in Hong Kong to become an artist. He falls in love with Suzie Wong, a rather young Eurasian prostitute. To be able to marry her, he goes to Macau, “where everything seems easier”. After all, as Anne Marie Evans reads in the book, it is “very easy” to do it in Macau, as long as you “bribe the right official”. At the time the book's action takes place, “there is nothing in Macau to keep it alive, except opium, gambling and girls”, noting that all of these are easily accessible at the Hotel Central where they will stay. In fact, adds French, the action is at the Hotel, but also on Rua da Felicidade. “It changes easily”, he concludes. Opened in the 1920s and after a few years of some decay, the Hotel Central reopened in 2024, after extensive renovation. “When Ian Fleming came here, the Hotel Central was no longer that big and continued to fall, but today we are here, on the terrace, with a new generation”, concluded Paul French, at the end of this literary foray.

item.title: Macau gaming sector EBITDA expected to accelerate in 2026
item.description: The growth of earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) in the Macau gaming sector is expected to accelerate in 2026, estimates investment bank UBS, which says that one of the main factors for this is the “stable competitive environment” and the fact that operators “increase focus on cost optimization”. In a note cited by the GGRAsia portal, UBS estimates that the gross gaming revenues of Macau casinos for the year 2026 will show a growth of 5% compared to the previous year, while the sector's adjusted EBITDA could increase by around 6% compared to 2025. UBS expects growth in gaming revenue to be driven by the 'premium' segment, supported by the sector's "marketing initiatives", as well as a greater supply of hotel rooms and the "optimization of reinvestment strategies" by operators. The investment bank's projection for the Macau gaming sector's adjusted EBITDA in 2026 was, however, reduced by around 2% compared to the previous estimate. UBS justifies this with the “highest” operating expenses in the sector in the fourth quarter of 2025. Still, UBS expects margins to remain stable in 2026, supported by an environment of “stable competition” and “cost optimization”. The bank also stated that this year “operators are shifting focus from improvements to facilities and additions of amenities to optimizing costs and reinvestment”. Additionally, gaming concessionaires are looking to improve the effectiveness of events.

item.title: Almost a third of Macau's elderly people participate in gambling
item.description:
The elderly in Macau see gambling as a way of exercising their autonomy and self-determination, in a phenomenon that mainly affects women, widowers and people with financial difficulties. A survey carried out in 2025, the results of which have now been revealed, indicates that 31.8% of elderly people in Macau participate in gambling and 2.6% suffer from addiction.
The percentage of elderly people participating in gambling-related activities in Macau was 31.8% in 2025, with 2.6% being pathological gamblers. These are the conclusions of a survey carried out by the Pathological Gambling Consultation Center Yat On, focusing on the gambling behaviors of the elderly population, their motivations and the potential risks of addiction. For this study, 616 responses were collected from people aged 55 or over, belonging to 12 day centers in Macau. The sample was mainly composed of women (89.4% of total respondents) aged 76 or over, who lived alone. Most of the participants in the study had low levels of education and received a monthly income of less than 6,533 patacas, as described by the newspaper Ou Mun. The survey's findings reveal that the elderly population prefers to play in casinos, followed by mahjong, slot machines and the Mark Six lottery. In addition to monetary factors, the main motivations for participating in the game concern the “search for new sensations” and relief from stress and the reinforcement of “self-esteem”, through the affirmation of personal capacity and self-determination. The risk of developing gambling disorders seems to be concentrated mainly at two crucial moments: the transition phase to retirement, between the ages of 55 and 60, and the period of loneliness at an older age, between the ages of 76 and 80. Elderly people who are single or widowed are particularly vulnerable groups to addiction, while the risk for those who live with children was estimated at 17%. In general, the elderly populations most susceptible to developing pathologies were women, widowers and low-income groups. In light of the survey's conclusions, the Yat On Pathological Gamblers' Consultation Center presented several recommendations specially designed to reduce the impacts of gambling on the health and well-being of the elderly. Firstly, and considering that gambling addiction is often associated with loneliness, it is proposed to reinforce social support through the creation of “alternative social platforms” for isolated women and elderly people and the expansion of cultural, sporting and creative offers available to the public. Mental health services must also be strengthened, offering support and emotional management courses so that older people can deal with loneliness and anxiety in more balanced ways. In the case of the most vulnerable groups, psychological counseling and behavioral therapy sessions may also be carried out. Regarding financial pressures, the center considers that a possible measure could include the “implementation of financial education” and the demystification of the idea that gambling is an effective way of earning or recovering money. Last year, a study by the University of Macau (UM) suggested that participation in the game among the elderly could help strengthen social connections and prevent cognitive decline in old age. However, researchers also highlighted the risks associated with problem gambling and urged Macau's casinos to “develop low-risk, highly social games that are more suitable for the territory's growing elderly population.”

item.title: Paradise Entertainment predicts 63.5% reduction in annual profits
item.description: Casino equipment maker Paradise Entertainment Ltd. forecasts profits of 139.4 million Hong Kong dollars for fiscal 2025, which represents a 63.5% drop from the previous year, the company said in a statement cited by the GGRAsia portal. According to the company, this forecast drop in profits is mainly due to a decrease of 85.2 million Hong Kong dollars, or 11.9% compared to the previous year, in revenues from the provision of casino management services in Macau; and a decrease of 165.5 million Hong Kong dollars, or 45.3% year-on-year, in revenue from sales and rental of electronic gaming equipment and systems. Another factor that reportedly had a negative impact on the company's profitability was a payment of HKD 42.1 million to its employees and the reimbursement to SJM Resorts Ltd. for the gaming operations team employed by SJM Resorts who worked at Kam Pek Paradise Casino. “The non-renewal of the service provision contract [with SJM Resorts] and the cessation of casino operations had a negative impact on the casino’s commercial performance,” Paradise Entertainment said in the note released on Monday. As the company explains, the early closure of casino operations “resulted in a loss of almost a month of revenue expected for the group” in 2025. Furthermore, the group saw a “drop in revenue from the sale and/or rental of electronic gaming equipment” last year, “particularly in relation to the sale of terminals and live gaming systems in Macau”.

item.title: New fund “opens doors” in China to Portuguese-speaking businesspeople, says Carlos Cid Álvares
item.description:
The president of the Portuguese-Chinese Chamber of Commerce and Industry (CCILC) in Macau yesterday told Lusa that a new investment fund launched by local authorities “opens doors” to Portuguese-speaking investors interested in China.
Carlos Cid Álvares pointed out that the 20 billion patacas fund “could be a significant step towards restructuring Macau's economic fabric”. The leader said that it represents “a paradigm shift”, as the city authorities begin to assume the role of “patient capital [investment that accepts a longer return] and guide” the diversification of the economy. The city government announced on February 27 the launch of a Government Guidance Fund, which aims to guide private capital for investment in priority sectors that help the local economy to diversify beyond the casino sector, dominant in Macau. The fund has four main targets: the health and wellness industry, the modern finance industry, the cutting-edge technology industry and finally the convention, exhibition and trade, culture and sports industry. Macau plans to complete the creation of the fund this year. “The focus on emerging industries and the transformation of scientific and technological results opens doors for foreign investors, including Portuguese speakers,” stated Álvares. For the president of CCILC, the fund, which is expected to start this year with an initial injection of 11 billion patacas, potentially reaching 20 billion patacas with private capital, “could be an opportunity for companies from Portuguese-speaking countries”. “The fund, by prioritizing investment in innovation and industrial modernization projects, could act as a catalyst for these companies to find a strategic partner and capital in Macau to scale their projects in Asia”, described Álvares. The business representative recalled that CCILC has defended Macau's role as a Sino-Lusophone platform and that this measure “realizes this vision in a practical way”. The businessman highlighted that the priority target sectors defined by the Macau Executive coincide with areas where companies from Brazil, Portugal and Portuguese-speaking African countries (PALOP) have “know-how and international competitiveness”. “Take the example of Brazil, with a strong presence in technological agribusiness and renewable energy, or Portugal, with a very dynamic ecosystem of technological startups and fintechs”, he pointed out. Another relevant point, according to the president of CCILC, is the articulation with the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin. The president of CCILC added that the fund “will provide liquidity and institutional weight” to the joint cooperation model established for Macau and Hengqin. “For Portuguese-speaking businesspeople, it could be the ideal scenario: installing their headquarters in Macau, benefiting from its legal system and familiarity with the Portuguese language, and locating the most space- or production-intensive component in Hengqin,” he explained. “We assume that the voice of experience from China and Portuguese-speaking markets can add value to the project”, concluded Álvares, also president of Banco Nacional Ultramarino, which is part of the Caixa Geral de Depósitos group. Lusa

item.title: Macau New District Secondary School in Hengqin starts operating next academic year
item.description: Macau Urban Renewal, the company responsible for the development of the New Macau Neighborhood in Hengqin, reported that the secondary school in that neighborhood had already passed the final inspection last month. Registration took place between the 1st and 8th of March, with the school expected to begin its activities in the 2026/2027 academic year. The school will have space for around 1,200 students. The school will offer residents of the Guangdong-Macao In-depth Cooperation Zone in Hengqin “comprehensive educational services” and “a high-quality learning environment in line with Macau standards,” says Macau Urban Renewal. The secondary school occupies an area of ​​approximately 20,000 square meters, with a total built area of ​​around 29,000 square meters. The school building has five floors and has an outdoor sports field, a multi-sports gym, a library, an auditorium, a science laboratory and multipurpose classrooms, among other facilities. With more than 4,000 residents, the New Macau Neighborhood in Hengqin “offers a full range of amenities,” says Macau Urban Renewal, highlighting public service facilities integrated into Macau's systems, such as a school, a health center, an elderly care center, a community service center for families and a 24-hour self-service center for Macau government affairs. Additionally, residents also have access to a residential parking lot, an outdoor playground, swimming pool, gym, tennis court, basketball court, pickleball court and a garden, “ensuring a comfortable and convenient living environment.”

item.title: Japan introduces electronic travel authorizations for 74 countries
item.description:
The Government of Japan yesterday approved a proposal to create an electronic travel authorization system for citizens of countries that currently do not require a visa for short stays, including Portugal and Brazil.
To come into force, the proposal must still be approved by Parliament (where the government coalition holds an absolute majority in the lower house, the most important), reported the Japanese news agency Kyodo. The system, inspired by the Electronic System for Travel Authorization in the United States, will have a fee to pay and will be applied to visitors and tourists from 74 countries exempt from short-term visas. The list includes Portugal, Brazil and the Chinese regions of Hong Kong and Macau, as well as some of the biggest tourist markets for Japan: South Korea, Taiwan and the United States. The Government will require airlines to deny boarding to travelers who do not have an electronic authorization. People visiting Japan for less than 90 days will be required to provide personal information online, as well as the purpose of the trip and place of accommodation. According to authorities, the system aims to prevent illegal work and terrorism. Additionally, the proposal includes a significant increase in visa renewal fees for foreign residents and for permanent residency applications. According to Kyodo, the current base fee for renewing temporary residence visas is six thousand yen (33 euros) and ten thousand yen (55 euros) for permanent residence applications. The proposed change raises the maximum renewal fee for temporary visas to 100,000 yen (546 euros) and for permanent residence to 300,000 yen (1,637 euros). “By requiring foreigners to bear a fair share of the costs necessary for the equitable management of entry, exit and residence, we intend to further strengthen and expand measures aimed at achieving an orderly and inclusive society,” explained Japanese Justice Minister Hiroshi Hiraguchi at a press conference, quoted by public broadcaster NHK. According to Kyodo, if the change is approved by Parliament, the new visa fees will come into force later this year, while the JESTA system will only come into effect in fiscal year 2028, which starts in April 2028 and runs until May 2029. The Conservative Government had already proposed a package of measures at the end of January to tighten immigration laws, including stricter requirements for obtaining Japanese citizenship. In addition to receiving a record number of 42.6 million tourists in 2025, Japan had almost four million foreign residents. The largest foreign community came from China, while Brazilians represented the sixth largest. Sectors of Japanese society criticized what they consider to be excessive permissiveness on the part of the authorities, which translated into electoral gains for openly xenophobic parties. Lusa

item.title: Casinos are earning 706 million patacas per day
item.description:
The gaming industry is showing good signs in the first days of March, indicates a report from Citigroup. According to the banking institution's accounts, casinos operating in Macau earned an average of around 706 million patacas per day in the first few days of this month, that is, 11% more than in March last year.
Casinos operating in Macau are showing a solid performance in the first days of March, indicates Citigroup in a report cited by the GGRAsia portal. The banking institution says that gross gaming revenues for the first eight days of this month reached around 5.65 billion patacas, which gives a daily average of around 706 million. This amount is around 11% higher than the daily average recorded in March 2025, which was 634 million patacas per day. Still, it is slightly below the average daily gaming revenue in February (737 million patacas per day). The institution also says that, at the beginning of March, the volume of VIP players fell by around 2% to 4% compared to last month. On the other hand, gross gaming revenue from the mass segment fell by around 3% to 5% compared to February. Citigroup says that one of the factors that contributed most to this good start to March for local casinos was the cultural offer provided, such as the concert by the Korean boy band Treasure, at the Venetian arena, the concert by the Chinese singer and composer Wang Sulong and the Korean female group Aespa at the Galaxy Arena. Despite the good start, the banking institution maintains its “conservative” forecast for gaming revenue for March at 22.5 billion patacas, which would represent an increase of 14% compared to the previous year. Citigroup expects average daily casino revenue to rise to 733 million patacas by the end of this month. In December last year, Citigroup suggested that more shows, an increase in the supply of hotel rooms and new side bets on baccarat could help boost gaming revenues throughout the year by around 6% year-on-year. It should be remembered that, in the month of February, casinos operating in Macau obtained total gross revenues of 20.6 billion patacas, that is, 4.5% more compared to February last year. There was, however, a drop of around 4% compared to January. Overall for the first two months of this year, gross gaming revenues reached 43.2 billion patacas, 13.9% more compared to the same period in 2025. Casinos operating in Macau obtained gross revenues of 247.4 billion patacas in 2025. This figure is 9.1% higher than the 226.7 billion registered in 2024. For this year, the Government believes that gross gaming revenue could reach 236 billion patacas. The consultancy Seaport recently indicated that the first quarter of the gaming industry is expected to register an increase of around 14% compared to the same period last year and 7% for the whole of 2026. Forecasts made by other consultants, such as Deutsche Bank and JP Morgan, point to a growth in gross revenues of Macau casinos in 2026 of around 5%.

item.title: Shares of Chinese battery maker CATL rise 10% as AI increases
item.description:
CATL shares soared after the world's largest battery maker reported better-than-expected earnings, with strong demand for energy storage systems, including for artificial intelligence data centers.
Shares of the Chinese company listed in Hong Kong rose up to 9.7% yesterday, after the company reported an annual net profit of 72.2 billion yuan on Monday night, 42% higher than in 2024 and above analysts' estimates. Its shares on the Shenzhen stock exchange rose up to 6.2%. The results highlight the Chinese battery maker's role in the global transition from fossil fuel-based energy and transport systems to electric, at a time when global oil and gas supply chains are shaken by conflict in the Middle East. The main stock markets in Hong Kong and mainland China rose around 1% yesterday, as oil prices fell following comments by United States President Donald Trump that the war in Iran would end “very soon”. CATL's revenue in 2025 was 17% higher than the previous year, reaching 423.7 billion yuan, as energy storage sales, including for power grids and AI data centers, increased 9% and overseas sales increased 18%. The company stated that “in international markets, increased energy demand from data centers and the need for flexible regulation of resources” have driven the growth of batteries for energy storage systems. CATL, which raised $5.3 billion last year in a secondary listing in Hong Kong, announced on Monday that it plans to issue up to 40 billion yuan in bonds as it continues to expand its international business, including new factories in Germany, Hungary, Indonesia and Spain. Electric vehicle battery revenue rose 25% last year, well above many analysts' estimates and amid expectations for a slower pace of growth in China, the world's biggest electric vehicle market, this year. While the group's global market share in electric vehicle batteries has reached a record 39%, the company faces long-term challenges due to US moves to block China from North American supply chains. The EU is also developing a regulatory framework to increase local components in manufacturing, a move that threatens to erode the margins of Chinese groups' low-cost operations in the region. Citi bank predicted that CATL sales will grow 31% in 2026, with sales of energy storage batteries increasing 45% and sales of batteries for electric vehicles increasing 27%. Lusa
